section.title: Freedom in Christ
section.sectionRationale: The doctrinal argument becomes a ringing call to action. It is for freedom that Christ has set us free, so the Galatians must stand firm and not take on the yoke of slavery. To be circumcised to be justified is to be severed from Christ and fallen from grace; what counts is faith working through love. Freedom is not license but the freedom to serve one another in love, fulfilling the law in a single command.
section.thesis: Christ has set us free for freedom, so believers must stand firm and refuse the yoke of the law, for seeking justification by law severs one from Christ and falls from grace; true freedom is not a license for the flesh but faith working through love, serving one another and fulfilling the whole law in the command to love your neighbor.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to stand firm in freedom and the warning that circumcision for justification severs from Christ; faith working through love as what truly counts, and the warning against the agitators; and freedom rightly used, serving one another in love and fulfilling the law.

units.label: Stand firm in freedom
units.note: do not take on the yoke of slavery

verses.text: It is for {{eleutheria|freedom}} that Christ has set us free. Stand firm, then, and do not be encumbered once more by a yoke of slavery.
Take notice: I, Paul, tell you that if you let yourselves be circumcised, Christ will be of no value to you at all.
Again I testify to every man who gets himself circumcised that he is obligated to obey the whole law.
You who are trying to be justified by the law have been severed from Christ; you have fallen away from {{charis|grace}}.

units.label: Faith working through love
units.note: what truly counts, and a warning to the agitators

verses.text: But by faith we eagerly await through the Spirit the hope of righteousness.
For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value. What matters is faith expressing itself through {{agape|love}}.
You were running so well. Who has obstructed you from obeying the truth?
Such persuasion does not come from the One who calls you.
A little {{zume|leaven}} works through the whole batch of dough.
I am confident in the Lord that you will take no other view. The one who is troubling you will bear the judgment, whoever he may be.
Now, brothers, if I am still preaching circumcision, why am I still being persecuted? In that case the offense of the cross has been abolished.
As for those who are agitating you, I wish they would proceed to emasculate themselves!

units.label: Freedom to serve in love
units.note: the whole law fulfilled in one command

verses.text: For you, brothers, were called to freedom; but do not use your freedom as an opportunity for the flesh. Rather, serve one another in love.
The entire law is fulfilled in a single decree: “Love your neighbor as yourself.”
But if you keep on biting and devouring one another, watch out, or you will be consumed by one another.

eleutheria.gloss: Freedom, liberty. This is the banner of the whole letter: Christ has set us free, and the purpose of that freedom is freedom itself, life released from the law as a means of acceptance and from bondage to sin. Yet Paul is careful, freedom is not license; it is liberty to love and serve. To go back under the law is to trade this freedom for a yoke of slavery.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor. To try to be justified by the law is to fall away from grace, verse 4, abandoning the only ground on which anyone stands before God. Grace and law keeping as a way of salvation cannot be mixed; to choose one is to lose the other. The believer lives entirely within the sphere of grace.

agape.gloss: Love, self giving love that seeks the good of the other. Faith is not idle; it expresses itself through love, verse 6. And freedom is exercised precisely in serving one another in love, so that the whole law is fulfilled in loving your neighbor. Love is the shape true faith takes and the way freedom is rightly used.

zume.gloss: Leaven, yeast, a small agent that spreads through the whole lump of dough. Paul warns that the false teaching, though it may seem a small matter like circumcision, will work through the entire community and corrupt it. A little error about the gospel does not stay small; it permeates and ruins, which is why it must not be tolerated.

basic.context: Paul turns from argument to appeal. Christ has set the Galatians free, so they must stand firm and not put on the yoke of the law. If they get circumcised in order to be justified, they cut themselves off from Christ, because they are trusting law rather than grace. What counts is faith that works through love. Freedom is not a license to sin but the freedom to serve one another in love.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the pivot of the letter, from doctrine to life. The freedom Paul defended is now the freedom he commands them to protect and to use rightly. Read the stern warnings and the call to love together: the same gospel that frees from law frees for love.
basic.originalLanguages: The keyword is eleutheria, freedom, the freedom Christ won. To seek justification by law is to fall from charis, grace. What counts is faith working through agape, love. And the false teaching is like zume, leaven, a little of it spreading through the whole church.
basic.typology: The yoke of slavery recalls the bondage of Egypt, from which God freed His people to serve Him. Christ is the greater exodus, setting us free not to do as we please but to love. Loving your neighbor as the fulfillment of the law points to Christ, who fulfilled it perfectly in laying down His life.
basic.christ: Christ set us free, and to add law to Him is to lose Him: Christ will be of no value to those who seek justification by circumcision. The offense of the cross, that we are saved by Christ crucified alone and not by our works, is the very thing the agitators want to remove. Christ crucified is the ground of both our freedom and our love.
basic.god: God is the One who calls the Galatians, and the false persuasion does not come from Him. He is the God of grace from which they must not fall. The Spirit He gives is the power by which believers await the hope of righteousness and serve in love. God frees His people to love as He has loved them.
basic.crossReferences: John 8 says the Son sets us free and we are free indeed. Romans 13 says love is the fulfilling of the law. 1 Corinthians 5 uses the same image of a little leaven spreading through the lump.
basic.oneStory: God is a liberator. As He freed Israel from Egypt to worship Him, so Christ frees His people from sin and law to love. The story runs from bondage to freedom, and the freedom is completed not in self indulgence but in a community that serves one another in love, fulfilling the law God always intended.

context.literary: Chapter 5 marks the turn from the theological argument of chapters 3 and 4 to the practical exhortation of chapters 5 and 6. Verse 1 is the hinge, restating the theme of freedom as a command. The section mixes strong warning with confident hope and ends by grounding all of ethics in love, which the rest of the chapter develops.
context.historical: Circumcision was the decisive issue: to accept it as necessary for justification was to accept the whole system of law as the path to God. Paul's sharp words in verse 12 reflect how gravely he viewed the agitators' work. The warning against biting and devouring suggests the controversy was already dividing the churches.
context.canonical: The passage is the classic biblical charter of Christian freedom, balancing liberty against both legalism and license. Its summary of the law in the command to love the neighbor echoes Jesus and Leviticus and anchors Christian ethics in love empowered by the Spirit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the Galatians to stand firm in the freedom Christ won, to warn that seeking justification by law severs them from Christ and grace, and to define true freedom as faith working through love and serving one another, not as license for the flesh.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is heavily prescriptive: stand firm, do not submit to the yoke, serve one another in love. The description of falling from grace states the real consequence of trusting law, warning believers away from that path.

debates.question: What does it mean to be severed from Christ and fallen from grace?

views.label: A warning against trusting law instead of Christ
views.case: To seek justification by law is to abandon grace as the ground of standing; one cannot hold Christ and law keeping together as the basis of acceptance, so choosing law forfeits Christ.

views.label: A statement about the incompatibility of the two systems
views.case: Paul describes the logic of the two ways: grace and law as means of justification are mutually exclusive, so to embrace one is to leave the other.

debates.bearing: The verse solemnly warns that adding law keeping as a ground of salvation is not a minor adjustment but a departure from the grace of Christ; the call is to rest in Christ alone.

debates.question: How is freedom kept from becoming license?

views.label: Freedom is directed toward love and service
views.case: Paul immediately channels freedom into serving one another in love; the same Spirit who frees produces love, so liberty is fulfilled, not violated, in self giving.

views.label: Freedom is limited by the law of love
views.case: Some frame it as love setting boundaries on freedom; Paul rather sees love as the very purpose of freedom, the law fulfilled in loving the neighbor.

debates.bearing: Christian freedom is never a license for the flesh; it is liberty to love, and the mark of true freedom is a community that serves rather than consumes one another.

study.originalLanguages: The banner word is eleutheria, freedom, and Paul stakes everything on it: Christ set us free for freedom, so stand firm. Its opposite is a fall from charis, grace, which is exactly what happens to those who seek justification by the law, for grace and law keeping cannot both be the ground of acceptance. The positive shape of freedom is faith working through agape, love, self giving care for one another that fulfills the whole law. And the danger is pictured as zume, leaven, a little of the false teaching quietly spreading until it corrupts the whole church, which is why Paul refuses to tolerate it.

typology.fulfillment: Recalls Egypt and every bondage; Christ is the greater exodus who frees His people to serve God in love, not under compulsion.

typology.type: Love your neighbor fulfilling the law
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ, who perfectly fulfilled the law by laying down His life in love, and who empowers His people to love by His Spirit.

study.christ: Christ is the liberator who set us free, and He is so central that to add law to Him is to lose Him entirely: Christ will be of no value to those who seek justification by circumcision. The offense of the cross, that sinners are saved by Christ crucified alone and not by their works, is precisely what the agitators want to smooth away. But Christ crucified is the ground of both the believer's freedom and the love that freedom produces, for He first loved us.
study.god: God is the One who calls the Galatians into grace, and Paul insists the persuasion toward law does not come from Him. He is the God of grace from which they are in danger of falling, and the giver of the Spirit by whom they await the hope of righteousness. God frees His people not to indulge themselves but to love, reflecting His own self giving love. The whole passage guards the freeness of His grace and directs His people toward love.

crossReferences.note: If the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed, the freedom Paul proclaims.
Love is the fulfilling of the law, echoing verse 14.
A little leaven leavens the whole lump, the same warning as verse 9.
Love your neighbor as yourself, the command Paul cites as fulfilling the law.

study.oneStory: God is a liberator from beginning to end. As He brought Israel out of Egypt to worship Him, so Christ brings His people out of bondage to sin and law to live in freedom. Yet the goal of the exodus was always a people who love God and one another. The gospel completes the story: a free community that serves in love, fulfilling the law God always intended, empowered by His Spirit.

sermon.title: The Only Thing That Counts (Galatians 5:1 to 12)
sermon.why: Begg calls believers to stand firm in the freedom Christ won, warns that seeking justification by law severs one from Christ, and shows that what truly counts is faith expressing itself through love.

quiz.q: For what purpose has Christ set us free?

options.text: For freedom, so we should stand firm and not take on a yoke of slavery.
options.note: Verse 1 states the purpose and the command together.

options.text: So we can now do whatever we please.
options.note: Freedom is not license for the flesh; it is freedom to love and serve.

options.text: So we can finally earn our salvation.
options.note: The freedom is from earning; salvation is by grace.

options.text: So we can return to the law with new strength.
options.note: Returning to the law is the yoke of slavery Paul warns against.

quiz.q: What happens to those who seek to be justified by the law?

options.text: They are severed from Christ and have fallen from grace.
options.note: Verse 4 states the solemn consequence.

options.text: They become stronger and more mature believers.
options.note: The opposite; they cut themselves off from Christ.

options.text: They gain a second way to be saved.
options.note: There is no second way; law keeping forfeits grace.

options.text: They please God more than others.
options.note: They fall from grace rather than pleasing God.

quiz.q: What does Paul say truly counts in Christ Jesus?

options.text: Faith expressing itself through love.
options.note: Verse 6 makes faith working through love the mark of what matters.

options.text: Whether or not one is circumcised.
options.note: Paul says neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value.

options.text: Strict observance of religious days.
options.note: What counts is faith working through love, not observance.

options.text: Public reputation for holiness.
options.note: Reputation is not the point; faith active in love is.

quiz.q: Why does Paul mention a little leaven?

options.text: To warn that a small error about the gospel spreads through the whole church.
options.note: Verse 9 warns of the pervasive corruption of false teaching.

options.text: To praise the growth of the church.
options.note: The image is a warning about corruption, not growth.

options.text: To recommend adding a little law to grace.
options.note: The leaven is the false teaching he opposes, not a recommendation.

options.text: To describe how bread should be baked.
options.note: It is a spiritual warning, not a baking instruction.

quiz.q: How should believers use their freedom?

options.text: To serve one another in love, fulfilling the law.
options.note: Verses 13 and 14 direct freedom toward love.

options.text: As an opportunity to indulge the flesh.
options.note: Paul explicitly forbids using freedom for the flesh.

options.text: To ignore the needs of others.
options.note: Freedom is for serving others, not ignoring them.

options.text: To compete for status in the church.
options.note: That leads to biting and devouring, which Paul warns against.

questions: Christ set you free for freedom. Where are you tempted to put on a yoke of slavery again, whether through legalism or through sin?
Paul says what counts is faith working through love. How is your faith actually expressing itself in love this week?
A little leaven spreads through the whole lump. Where might a small compromise about the gospel be quietly working through your thinking or your community?
Freedom is not license but liberty to serve. Whom is God calling you to serve in love out of the freedom Christ has given?
Paul warns against biting and devouring one another. Where is conflict consuming a relationship, and how might love rather than winning change it?

translationNotes.note: It is for freedom that Christ has set us free renders an emphatic construction; freedom is both the source and the goal of Christ's work, and the verse turns it into a command to stand firm.
The offense of the cross is the stumbling block that salvation comes through Christ crucified alone, not through our works; removing it is exactly what the agitators attempt.
Love your neighbor as yourself quotes Leviticus 19:18; Paul says the whole law is fulfilled in this single command, echoing the teaching of Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 5:1-15. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg; the movement spans 5:1-15 while the sermon covers 5:1-12.
